items.text: Ask about onset, location, radiation, severity, timing, and relationship to meals, movement, or bowel movements.
Ask about oral intake, nausea, vomiting, fluid losses, urine output, and ability to tolerate medications.
Ask about stool frequency and character, flatus, bleeding, dysuria, hematuria, and change from baseline.
Ask about prior surgery, obstruction, gallstones, liver disease, ulcers, inflammatory bowel disease, and prior episodes.
Ask about NSAIDs, anticoagulants, alcohol, new medications, antibiotics, toxins, and relevant dietary exposures.
Ask about pregnancy possibility and gynecologic symptoms when clinically relevant.
Ask about cramping or constant pain, distention, vomiting, bilious or feculent emesis, and inability to pass stool or flatus.
Ask about prior abdominal or pelvic surgery, hernias, prior obstruction, inflammatory bowel disease, radiation, and malignancy.
Ask about fever, persistent focal pain, bloody stool, syncope, reduced urine output, and inability to tolerate oral intake.
Ask about opioids, anticholinergics, laxatives, baseline bowel pattern, constipation, and recent change in medications.
Assess appearance, vital signs, mucous membranes, orthostatic symptoms when safe, and bedside perfusion.
Inspect for scars, distention, asymmetry, visible hernia, skin changes, and respiratory movement of the abdomen.
Auscultate and palpate systematically for focal tenderness, guarding, rebound, mass, organ enlargement, or pulsatility.
Assess for costovertebral-angle tenderness and examine relevant hernia sites when indicated.
Perform rectal, perianal, or pelvic examination only when clinically indicated and appropriate.
Inspect for jaundice, pallor, edema, bruising, chronic liver-disease stigmata, and other systemic findings when relevant.
Inspect for distention, scars, asymmetry, visible peristalsis, hernias, and skin changes.
Auscultate bowel sounds and palpate for tenderness, guarding, rebound, rigidity, mass, and tympany.
Examine relevant inguinal, femoral, umbilical, and incisional hernia sites when appropriate.
Perform rectal examination when clinically indicated and appropriate to assess stool, mass, blood, or impaction.
